facts.fact_text: LVQ is an online algorithm—observations are processed one at a time.

The previous chapters have been concerned with predicting the values
of one or more outputs or response variables Y
= (Y1, . . . , Ym) for a
given set of input or predictor variables XT = (X1, . . . , Xp).

The predictions are based on the training sample
(x1, y1), . . . , (xN, yN) of previously solved cases, where the joint values of
all of the variables are known. This is called supervised learning or “learn-
ing with a teacher.”

In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.
The learning rate ϵ is decreased to zero with each iteration, following the guidelines for stochastic approximation learning rates (Section 11.4).

In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.

If one supposes that (X, Y ) are random variables represented by some
joint probability density Pr(X, Y ), then supervised learning can be formally
characterized as a density estimation problem where one is concerned with
determining properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X).

These classifiers are memory-based, and require no model to be fit. Given a query point x0, we find the k training points x(r), r = 1, . . . , k closest in distance to x0, and then classify using majority vote among the k neighbors.
\hat{G}(x) = \arg\max_j \sum_{i \in N_k(x)} I(y_i = j)

In low-dimensional problems (say p ≤3), there are a variety of eﬀective
nonparametric methods for directly estimating the density Pr(X) itself at
all X-values, and representing it graphically (Silverman, 1986, e.g.). Owing
to the curse of dimensionality, these methods fail in high dimensions.

With supervised learning there is a clear measure of success, or lack
thereof, that can be used to judge adequacy in particular situations and
to compare the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent methods over various situations.

ing with a teacher.”

In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.

One
must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian
mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).

These classifiers are memory-based, and require no model to be fit.

properties of interest are the “location” parameters μ that minimize the
expected error at each x,

Cluster anal-
ysis attempts to ﬁnd multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain
modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by
a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of ob-
servations.
For K = 2 the 1-nearest-neighbor error rate is 2pk∗(x)(1 −pk∗(x)) ≤2(1 −pk∗(x)) (twice the Bayes error rate).
E[\text{Error}_{1-NN}] \leq 2E[\text{Error}_{Bayes}]

In low-dimensional problems (say p ≤3), there are a variety of eﬀective
nonparametric methods for directly estimating the density Pr(X) itself at
all X-values, and representing it graphically (Silverman, 1986, e.g.). Owing
to the curse of dimensionality, these methods fail in high dimensions.

With supervised learning there is a clear measure of success, or lack
thereof, that can be used to judge adequacy in particular situations and
to compare the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent methods over various situations.

Here we develop a locally adaptive form of nearest-neighbor classification that tries to use class information to adaptively shape the metric in each neighborhood. This is done by shaping the neighborhood locally to concentrate on the dimensions in which the information about the class probabilities is most likely to be

In low-dimensional problems (say p ≤3), there are a variety of eﬀective
nonparametric methods for directly estimating the density Pr(X) itself at
all X-values, and representing it graphically (Silverman, 1986, e.g.). Owing
to the curse of dimensionality, these methods fail in high dimensions.

Principal
components, multidimensional scaling, self-organizing maps, and principal
curves, for example, attempt to identify low-dimensional manifolds within
the X-space that represent high data density.

If one supposes that (X, Y ) are random variables represented by some
joint probability density Pr(X, Y ), then supervised learning can be formally
characterized as a density estimation problem where one is concerned with
determining properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X).
The chi-square distance is a natural choice for discrete distributions. If p = (p1, p2, . . . , pm) and q = (q1, q2, . . . , qm) are two discrete distributions, the chi-square distance is
d_{\chi^2}(p, q) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{(p_j - q_j)^2}{p_j + q_j}
Most algorithms use dissimilarities d(xi, xi′) or similarities s(xi, xi′) between pairs of observations.

Cluster anal-
ysis attempts to ﬁnd multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain
modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by
a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of ob-
servations.

Market basket analysis is the prototypical example of unsupervised learning. It is a method for finding relationships between items in large databases of customer transactions. The idea is to discover what items or products are purchased together.

Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued
data.

In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.

One
must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian
mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).
Generally, these descriptive statistics attempt to characterize X-values,
or collections of such values, where Pr(X) is relatively large.

An association rule is an implication expression of the form X ⇒ Y, where X and Y are disjoint itemsets. The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y. The rule has confidence c if c% of transactions that contain X also contain Y.
X \Rightarrow Y

Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued
data.

Cluster anal-
ysis attempts to ﬁnd multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain
modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by
a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of ob-
servations. Mixture modeling has a similar goal.

In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.
The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y.
\text{supp}(X \Rightarrow Y) = P(X \cup Y)
The Apriori algorithm is an efficient bottom-up algorithm for mining all itemsets that satisfy a minimum support threshold.
An association rule is an implication expression of the form X ⇒ Y, where X and Y are disjoint itemsets.
The rule has confidence c if c% of transactions that contain X also contain Y.
\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = P(Y|X)
The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y. The rule has confidence c if c% of transactions that contain X also contain Y.
The lift of a rule is defined as the confidence divided by the support of Y (the baseline frequency): lift(X ⇒ Y) = conf(X ⇒ Y)/supp(Y).

The Apriori algorithm is an efficient bottom-up algorithm for mining all itemsets that satisfy a minimum support threshold. It exploits the downward closure property: if an itemset is frequent, then all of its subsets must also be frequent.
It exploits the downward closure property: if an itemset is frequent, then all of its subsets must also be frequent.

Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued
data.

Association rules attempt

It exploits the downward closure property: if an itemset is frequent, then all of its subsets must also be frequent.
The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y.

Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued
data.
The lift of a rule is defined as the confidence divided by the support of Y (the baseline frequency): lift(X ⇒ Y) = conf(X ⇒ Y)/supp(Y).
\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{supp}(Y)}
The rule has confidence c if c% of transactions that contain X also contain Y.
The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y.

Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued
data.
Generalized association rules allow for quantitative and categorical variables. The basic strategy is to discretize these variables and then apply a standard association rule mining algorithm.

An association rule is an implication expression of the form X ⇒ Y, where X and Y are disjoint itemsets. The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y. The rule has confidence c if c% of transactions that contain X also contain Y.

to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued

In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.

Unsupervised learning problems can be recast as supervised learning problems. The idea is to create a two-class response: for each itemset, code each transaction as 1 if it contains the itemset, and 0 otherwise. Then use a supervised learning method to predict the response from the other features.

characterized as a density estimation problem where one is concerned with
determining properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X).

With supervised learning there is a clear measure of success, or lack

to compare the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent methods over various situations.

For data with p quantitative features, Euclidean distance is the most common choice. Other choices include L1 (Manhattan) distance and Lq distance for general q.
